fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that BDD step-wiring utility we talked about in the last planning session. Basically the idea is: developers write plain English scenarios and we need a tool that can hook those up to actual code. There are a few moving parts here.

First, when a step doesn't have a definition yet, the tool should spit out a ready-to-paste code stub — like, automatically handle all the annoying escaping so devs don't have to do it by hand. We had a similar escaping approach in the old regex helper module, so let's stay consistent with that.

Second, we need pattern matching that can pull out the actual argument values from a step sentence and tell you where in the string they appeared.

Third, there should be a registry where you can load up a bunch of patterns and run a step description against all of them at once.

Fourth, the tabular data attached to steps needs to be buildable in a structured way — it should blow up early if someone tries to do something invalid like adding a row before setting up columns, or mismatching the row width.

Lastly, we need tag filtering — both the "any of these" and "all of these" flavors. The AND version has some quoting syntax I can't quite remember the exact rules for, so double-check how the sub-expressions are delimited.

Please make sure the whole thing is clean and testable with no I/O side effects in the core logic.

A couple follow-ups from the questions that came in. On the escaping piece, the regex escaping is only for these characters: `|`, `(`, `)`, `[`, `]`, `{`, `}`, `^`, `$`, `*`, `+`, `?`, `.`, and `\`. Everything else should pass through unchanged. And for snippet generation, it’s definitely a two-step thing on the step text: first escape every regex metacharacter so it matches literally, then take that already-escaped result and escape every double-quote and every backslash again so it’s safe inside a double-quoted C-style string literal. After that, wrap the whole thing with `^` and `$`. Same two-pass behavior should stay consistent anywhere we’re doing that snippet-style escaping flow.

Also, the generated stub shape should be exact. The keyword is uppercased, and the first line should look like `THEN("^...$") {`. After that it’s a newline, then four spaces followed by `pending();`, then a newline, then a closing brace `}`, then a final newline. So the body is exactly that formatting, not just roughly similar.

For `regex_find_all`, scanning should be consecutive and non-overlapping starting from the beginning of the string. We only report the first capturing group from each token match. The first output line is `matched=true` if we found at least one token, otherwise `matched=false`. Then the token values come out as `token[0]=...`, `token[1]=...` and so on. On positions, the `position` for each captured group is the zero-based character offset in the full input string, not relative to the match start and not relative to any other group. Groups start at 0 and should be reported in pattern order.

One other important boundary line: the core business logic needs zero dependency on stdin/stdout or JSON parsing. The execution adapter layer is the only thing that should read JSON commands from stdin, turn those into method calls on the core domain objects, and write formatted results to stdout. Core domain stays fully testable with no I/O.

And on the AND tag syntax I was fuzzy about earlier, the format is the one where each sub-expression is wrapped in double-quotes and separated by commas, like `"@a,@b", "@c", "@d,@e,@f"`.

trigger_keywords: step registry plain text matching
how does a definition with no capture groups match
substring vs full match in step registry
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In the step registry, a definition pattern is used as a regex and a step description matches if the pattern is found anywhere within the description (substring/search match, not a full-string match). If the pattern has no capturing groups the match is still reported but with `args=0` and no `arg[N]` lines. Duplicate registrations each produce their own independent match entry.

trigger_keywords: table column ordering in output
how are columns sorted in row output
what order do column names appear
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When reporting table rows, column names are sorted lexicographically (alphabetical/sorted order) and each column's name=value pair appears in that sorted order on the row line. The row index is zero-based.

trigger_keywords: table error categories
what errors can the table builder report
invalid table operation error names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: There are exactly three error categories the table builder can report: `row_size_mismatch` (a row's cell count differs from the number of declared columns), `no_columns` (a row is appended before any column has been declared), and `column_after_rows` (a column is declared after at least one row has already been appended). On any error, processing halts immediately and outputs `error=<category>`.

trigger_keywords: AND tag expression quoting syntax
how are sub-expressions delimited in conjunctive tags
tag_and expression format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In a conjunctive (`tag_and`) expression the sub-expressions are comma-separated and each sub-expression is wrapped in double-quotes, e.g. `"@a,@b", "@c"`. Each quoted sub-expression is itself a disjunctive group: its comma-separated tokens are OR-ed together. The whole expression matches only when every quoted sub-expression matches. An empty expression (no sub-expressions) matches every tag list.

trigger_keywords: OR tag expression empty case
what happens when tag_or expression is empty
disjunctive tag with no tokens
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An empty disjunctive (`tag_or`) expression — one with no tokens — matches NO tag list (always false). In contrast, an empty conjunctive (`tag_and`) expression matches EVERY tag list (always true). This asymmetry is intentional.

trigger_keywords: tag token matching with at-sign
do tag tokens include the @ prefix
how are expression tokens compared to candidate tags
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Tag expression tokens may include an `@` prefix (e.g. `@a`), but the candidate tag lists contain tags WITHOUT the `@` prefix (e.g. `"a"`). Matching strips or ignores the `@` from the expression token when comparing against the candidate list, so `@a` in the expression matches `a` in the candidate list.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'snippet', 'escape_regex', 'escape_cstring', 'regex_find', 'regex_find_all', 'step_match', 'table', 'tag_or', 'tag_and'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: blow up early if someone tries to do something invalid
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The three specific DataTable error categories in Feature 4: `row_size_mismatch`, `no_columns` (row before any column declared), and `column_after_rows` (column declared after rows exist) — defined in feature4_table.json.

context_pointers.vague_reference: tell you where in the string they appeared
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The zero-based absolute character offset (`position`) of each captured group within the full input string, as reported in the `submatch[N] value=... position=...` output format of Feature 2.1.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but I don't have the specific details on that aspect. I'd suggest checking the test case files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` for the relevant feature — the expected input/output pairs there are the authoritative spec for formatting and edge-case behavior.

feature1_1_snippet.json.description: Given a step keyword and the literal text of an undefined step, produce a ready-to-paste step-definition stub. The stub uppercases the keyword, then on the first line emits the keyword followed by, in parentheses and double quotes, an anchored pattern built from the step text: a leading caret, the step text with every regular-expression metacharacter backslash-escaped and then every double-quote and backslash escaped again for embedding inside a C-style string literal, and a trailing dollar sign. The body is a fixed two-line block that marks the step as pending.

feature1_2_regex_escape.json.description: Given an arbitrary literal string, return the string with every regular-expression metacharacter escaped by a preceding backslash so the result matches the original text literally when used as a pattern. The metacharacters that get escaped are the alternation bar, parentheses, square brackets, curly braces, caret, dollar, star, plus, question mark, dot and backslash; all other characters pass through unchanged.

feature1_3_cstring_escape.json.description: Given an arbitrary literal string, return the string escaped for safe embedding inside a double-quoted C-style string literal: every double-quote character and every backslash character is prefixed with a backslash, while all other characters pass through unchanged.

feature2_1_regex_find.json.description: Match a regular expression against an input string, reporting whether it matched and, when it did, the value and zero-based character position of each captured group in the order they appear. The first output line states whether a match occurred. When the pattern contains no capturing groups a successful match reports the match only; when it contains capturing groups each captured substring is reported with its absolute position in the input. A failed match reports no captured groups.

cases.expected_output:
matched=true
submatch[0] value=42 position=0
submatch[1] value=69 position=6

feature2_2_regex_find_all.json.description: Scan an input string from the beginning for consecutive non-overlapping tokens that each match the supplied pattern, and report the value of the first capturing group of every token in order. The first output line states whether at least one token was found. When the input yields no tokens, no token values are reported; otherwise each token's first-group value is listed in order of appearance.

feature3_step_match.json.description: Register an ordered list of step-definition matcher patterns, then match a single step description against all of them. Report how many definitions matched and, for each match in registration order, the zero-based index of the matching definition together with the count and the value and absolute position of every captured argument. A plain-text matcher matches a description that contains it; a matcher with capturing groups extracts the corresponding argument values and positions. Identical matchers registered more than once each produce their own match. Matchers and descriptions may contain non-ASCII text, which is compared literally.

cases.expected_output:
matches=1
def[1] args=1
arg[0] value=first position=10

feature4_table.json.description: Build a data table by applying an ordered sequence of operations that either declare a column or append a row of cell values, then report the table contents. A row may only be appended after at least one column is declared and must supply exactly one value per declared column; columns may not be declared after any row has been appended. On success the number of rows is reported followed by one line per row pairing each column name with its cell value (column names in sorted order). A violated rule stops processing and reports a neutral error category instead: a row whose width does not match the number of columns, a row appended before any column is declared, or a column declared after rows already exist.

feature5_1_or_tags.json.description: Evaluate a disjunctive tag expression against one or more candidate tag lists. The expression is a comma-separated list of tag tokens (optionally spaced) and matches a tag list when at least one of its tokens is present in that list. An empty expression matches no tag list. For each candidate tag list the comma-joined tags and whether the expression matched are reported, one line per candidate.

cases.expected_output:
query=a matched=true
query=b matched=true
query=a,b matched=true
query=x,a,b matched=true
query=x,y,z matched=false

feature5_2_and_tags.json.description: Evaluate a conjunctive tag expression against one or more candidate tag lists. The expression is a comma-separated list of quoted sub-expressions (optionally spaced), and matches a tag list only when every sub-expression matches it; each quoted sub-expression is itself a disjunctive (comma-separated) group that matches when any of its tokens is present. An empty expression matches every tag list. For each candidate tag list the comma-joined tags and whether the expression matched are reported, one line per candidate.

cases.expected_output:
query=a,b matched=true
query=x,a,b matched=true
query=a matched=false
query=b matched=false
query=a,y matched=false
query=x,b matched=false
query=x,y matched=false
